chunk_summary: This section focuses on BMI-for-age classification for diagnosing and managing thinness in individuals aged 5-19 years, highlighting differential diagnosis and program linkages to address moderate acute malnutrition (MAM). It references WHO growth standards and national frameworks for adolescent nutrition and supplementary feeding programs.
learning_objectives: Understand the BMI-for-age classification system for ages 5-19 years based on WHO growth standards.
Identify differential diagnoses for thinness in adolescents and children.
Explore programmatic linkages between MAM management and national nutrition initiatives.
Learn about operational frameworks like RKSK, WIFS, and PM POSHAN for addressing malnutrition.
key_facts: BMI-for-age is a key indicator for assessing thinness in individuals aged 5-19 years, with WHO cut-offs defining moderate and severe thinness.
The RKSK framework emphasizes adolescent health, including nutrition and anemia prevention.
The WIFS program targets weekly iron and folic acid supplementation for adolescents to combat anemia.
PM POSHAN (formerly Mid-Day Meal Scheme) provides nutritional support to school-aged children to improve dietary intake.
Differential diagnosis for thinness includes ruling out chronic infections, eating disorders, and socioeconomic factors.
reference_quote: "BMI-for-age (5-19 years): interpretation of cut-offs (WHO growth reference)" — https://www.who.int/tools/growth-reference-data-for-5to19-years/indicators/bmi-for-age
document_key_topics: National nutrition frameworks (RKSK, WIFS, PM POSHAN)
